author: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy
book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

lineage: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.

From the Definitions Given of Sin and Will, He Overthrows the Entire Heresy of the Manichæans. Likewise from the Just Condemnation of Evil Souls It Follows that They are Evil Not by Nature But by Will. That Souls are Good By Nature, to Which the Pardon of Sins is Granted.

metadata.author: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy

ccel.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

source.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

title: From the Definitions Given of Sin and Will, He Overthrows the Entire Heresy of the Manichæans. Likewise from the Just Condemnation of Evil Souls It Follows that They are Evil Not by Nature But by Will. That Souls are Good By Nature, to Which the Pardon of Sins is Granted.

source.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: Come now, let us see in what respect these things would have aided us.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: Much every way, so that I should have desired nothing more; for they end the whole cause; for whoever consulting in the inner mind, where they are more pronounced and assured, the secrets of his own conscience, and the divine laws absolutely imposed upon nature, grants that these two definitions of will and sin are true, condemns without any hesitation by the fewest and the briefest, but plainly the most invincible reasons, the whole heresy of the Manichæans.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: Which can be thus considered.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: They say that there are two kinds of souls, the one good, which is in such a way from God, that it is said not to have been made by Him out of any material or out of nothing, but to have proceeded as a certain part from the very substance itself of God; the other evil, which they believe and strive to get others to believe pertains to God in no way whatever; and so they maintain that the one is the perfection of good, but the other the perfection of evil, and that these two classes were at one time distinct but are now commingled.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: The character and the cause of this commingling I had not yet heard; but nevertheless I could have inquired whether that evil kind of souls, before it was mingled with the good, had any will.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: For if not, it was without sin and innocent, and so by no means evil.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: But if evil in such a way, that though without will, as fire, yet if it should touch the good it would violate and corrupt it; how impious it is to believe that the nature of evil is powerful enough to change any part of God, and that the Highest Good is corruptible and violable!

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: But if the will was present, assuredly there was present, no one compelling, a movement of the mind either towards not losing something or obtaining something.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: But this something was either good, or was thought to be good, for not otherwise could it be earnestly desired.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: But in supreme evil, before the commingling which they maintain, there never was any good.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: Whence then could there be in it either the knowledge or the thought of good?

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: Did they wish for nothing that was in themselves, and earnestly desire that true good which was without?

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: That will must truly be declared worthy of distinguished and great praise by which is earnestly desired the supreme and true good.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: Whence then in supreme evil was this movement of mind most worthy of so great praise?

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: Did they seek it for the sake of injuring it?

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: In the first place, the argument comes to the same thing.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: For he who wishes to injure, wishes to deprive another of some good for the sake of some good of his own.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: There was therefore in them either a knowledge of good or an opinion of good, which ought by no means to belong to supreme evil.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: In the second place, whence had they known, that good placed outside of themselves, which they designed to injure, existed at all.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: If they had intellectually perceived it, what is more excellent than such a mind?

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: Is there anything else for which the whole energy of good men is put forth except the knowledge of that supreme and sincere good?

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: What therefore is now scarcely conceded to a few good and just men, was mere evil, no good assisting, then able to accomplish?

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: But if those souls bore bodies and saw the supreme good with their eyes, what tongues, what hearts, what intellects suffice for lauding and proclaiming those eyes, with which the minds of just men can scarcely be compared?

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: How great good things we find in supreme evil!

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: For if to see God is evil, God is not a good; but God is a good; therefore to see God is good; and I know not what can be compared to this good.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: Since to see anything is good, whence can it be made out that to be able to see is evil?

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: Therefore whatever in those eyes or in those minds brought it about, that the divine essence could be seen by them, brought about a great thing and a good thing most worthy of ineffable praise.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: But if it was not brought about, but it was such in itself and eternal, it is difficult to find anything better than this evil.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: Lastly, that these souls may have nothing of these praiseworthy things which by the reasonings of the Manichæans they are compelled to have, I should have asked, whether God condemns any or no souls.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: If none, there is no judgment of rewards and punishments, no providence, and the world is administered by chance rather than by reason, or rather is not administered at all.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: For the name administration must not be given to chances.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: But if it is impious for all those that are bound by any religion to believe this, it remains either that there is condemnation of some souls, or that there are no sins.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: But if there are no sins, neither is there any evil.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: Which if the Manichæans should say, they would slay their heresy with a single blow.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: Therefore they and I agree that some souls are condemned by divine law and judgment.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: But if these souls are good, what is that justice?

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: If evil, are they so by nature, or by will?

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: But by nature souls can in no way be evil.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: Whence do we teach this.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: From the above definitions of will and sin.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: For to speak of souls, and that they are evil, and that they do not sin, is full of madness; but to say that they sin without will, is great craziness, and to hold any one guilty of sin for not doing what he could not do, belongs to the height of iniquity and insanity.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: Wherefore whatever these souls do, if they do it by nature not by will, that is, if they are wanting in a movement of mind free both for doing and not doing, if finally no power of abstaining from their work is conceded to them; we cannot hold that the sin is theirs.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: But all confess both that evil souls are justly, and souls that have not sinned are unjustly condemned; therefore they confess that those souls are evil that sin.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: But these, as reason teaches, do not sin.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: Therefore the extraneous class of evil souls of the Manichæans, whatever it may be, is a non-entity.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: Let us now look at that good class of souls, which again they exalt to such a degree as to say that it is the very substance of God.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: But how much better it is that each one should recognize his own rank and merit, nor be so puffed up with sacrilegious pride as to believe that as often as he experiences a change in himself it is the substance of that supreme good, which devout reason holds and teaches to be unchangeable!

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: For behold! since it is manifest that souls do not sin in not being such as they cannot be; it follows that these supposititious souls, whatever they may be, do not sin at all, and moreover that they are absolutely non-existent; it remains that since there are sins, they find none to whom to attribute them except the good class of souls and the substance of God.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: But especially are they pressed by Christian authority; for never have they denied that forgiveness of sins is granted when any one has been converted to God; never have they said (as they have said of many other passages) that some corrupter has interpolated this into the divine Scriptures.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: To whom then are sins attributed?

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: If to those evil souls of the alien class, these also can become good, can possess the kingdom of God with Christ.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: Which denying, they [the Manichæans] have no other class except those souls which they maintain are of the substance of God.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: It remains that they acknowledge that not only these latter also, but these alone sin.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: But I make no contention about their being alone in sinning; yet they sin.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: But are they compelled to sin by being commingled with evil?

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: If so compelled that there was no power of resisting, they do not sin.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: If it is in their power to resist, and they voluntarily consent, we are compelled to find out through their [the Manichæan] teaching, why so great good things in supreme evil, why this evil in supreme good, unless it be that neither is that which they bring into suspicion evil, nor is that which they pervert by superstition supreme good?
